mappings.rationaleAI: The core article covering all aspects of the section outline: diverse conceptions of music as art, musical meaning, performance, notation, all formal elements (pitch, rhythm, harmony, counterpoint, texture, form, timbre), instruments, vocal music, genres, recording technology, and the full history of Western and world music.

Directly addresses the human voice as source of musical sound (B.2), covering vocal classification, registration, technique, and the art of singing across Western and non-Western genres from classical art song and opera to folk and popular music.

Covers musical theatre (including opera) as one of the principal genres of music for the theatre (E.3.c.iii) and situates opera within the broader theatrical tradition (E.3.c.i), connecting music to the performing arts context.

Bach's encyclopedic mastery of counterpoint and fugue directly illustrates C.5 and E.2.a.v; his sacred cantatas, Passions, and motets exemplify E.1.b.ii and E.3.a; his career represents a foundational chapter in Western music history (G.1).

Beethoven's symphonies, concertos, and piano sonatas directly exemplify the principal notated instrumental forms (E.2.a.i-iii) and his string quartets exemplify chamber music (E.3.b); his career spans the Classical-to-Romantic transition in Western music history (G.1).
Directly addresses popular music as a mass-culture phenomenon, situating it within the commercial media system that shapes the production and consumption of popular and folk genres treated under E.4.

Dance is the primary context for music for theatrical dance (ballet, modern dance) addressed under E.3.c.ii, and the article covers music's role as the temporal organizer and expressive partner of choreographic form.

Opera is the paradigmatic genre of music for the theatre (E.3.c.i), and the article's coverage of vocal forms — recitative, aria, bel canto — directly addresses B.2 (the human voice and singing styles) and solo vocal performance (E.1.b.i).

Situates music within the broader context of performing arts (A.4, the relation of music to other human activities), and its historical survey of musical and theatrical traditions across cultures contributes to the comparative dimension of sections E and G.

Directly covers the history of music from prehistoric origins through ancient, medieval, Renaissance, Baroque, Classical, Romantic, and modern periods (G, G.1), and addresses debates about music's origins and cross-cultural universality relevant to A.1 and E.4.

Directly covers rhythm, pulse, beat, measure, tempo, and metre as distinct but interrelated musical elements (C.2, C.2.a-c), including cross-cultural variation from African polyrhythm to Indian tala systems and Western mensural notation.

The orchestra as a large instrumental ensemble directly addresses B.3 (musical instruments by class) and C.7 (orchestration and instrumentation); its repertoire spanning symphonies and concertos connects to E.2.a.ii and chamber/ensemble distinctions to E.3.b.
Motion pictures are one of the principal contexts for incidental and background music addressed under E.3.c.iv, with film scoring representing a major genre of 20th-century music production.

Armstrong is the defining figure of early jazz performance, directly illustrating the jazz section's emphasis on improvisation, the performer as creator, and the social/cultural forces behind jazz (E.2.b.i); his career traces the development of jazz styles (E.2.b.ii).

The Beatles represent the paradigmatic example of popular music (E.4) and their pioneering use of multi-track studio recording directly addresses the techniques of music recording (F.2) and effects on music production and criticism (F.3, F.4) in Western music history (G.1).

Pop music is directly addressed as a genre under E.4 (popular music), covering its hook-driven structures, commercial character, and global variants (K-pop, J-pop), while its mid-20th-century emergence contributes to the contemporary section of G.1.

Directly addresses all classes of musical instruments using the Hornbostel-Sachs classification system that maps onto B.3.a (idiophones/membranophones), B.3.b (aerophones), B.3.c (chordophones), and B.3.d (electrophones), covering history, technology, and the study of organology.

Covers the full arc of Western art music (G.1) from plainchant through Baroque counterpoint (C.5), Classical harmony (C.4), and sophisticated written notation (D), with its genres spanning sacred (E.3.a), chamber (E.3.b), and operatic (E.3.c.i) forms.
Television is a primary medium for incidental and background music (E.3.c.iv) and its broadcast technology represents a major form of music reproduction and transmission (F.1).

Problems of musical meaning (A.2) are centrally concerned with how music expresses, evokes, or represents emotion; the article's coverage of emotional theories (dimensional models, somatic theories) informs diverse conceptions of music as an art (A.1).

Directly addresses writings about music (A.5) by defining musicology's three branches — historical, systematic, and ethnomusicological — which encompass the history of music (G), music theory, performance study (A.3), and diverse aesthetic conceptions (A.1).

Rock music is a major genre under E.4 (popular music), whose history from 1950s rock and roll through numerous subgenres constitutes an important chapter of G.1; its development is deeply tied to recorded music's effects on composition and culture (F.3, F.4).

The progression from phonograph to radio to digital transmission constitutes the history of music recording and reproduction (F, F.1, F.4), as telecommunications technology — telephone, radio, internet — fundamentally shaped how music is distributed and consumed.

Folklore's coverage of oral tradition, folk song, and the informal community-based transmission of musical culture directly addresses the folk and primitive music genres under E.4, including ballads, work songs, and ceremonial music.

Oral tradition encompasses the pre-notational transmission of music — epic poetry with melodic formula, ballads, religious chant — directly relevant to E.4 (primitive and folk music) and to understanding the limits and antecedents of musical notation (D).
